titleStmt.title: Letter from Katie Mooney to her brother Peter Mooney, 2 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Katie Mooney

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
A letter from Katie Mooney (b.1884) to her brother Peter Mooney (b.1886). Katie sends Peter money, socks and tobacco alongside the letter. Katie writes that she would pray to God to protect him. She explains that she wrote her address twice as she feared that the letter wouldn't reach her brother or that it would be misplaced.
Orphaned at age 7, Peter and his sister Katie were raised by their uncle William and his wife Mary in Caulstown. While there are no surviving enlistment papers for Peter, his War Badge Index Record shows his enlistment date as 18 October 1915. He served with the Royal Artillery and later transferred to the Royal Engineers where he was promoted to the rank of Sergeant. He served in several places at the Western Front, including Ypres, St. Omer and Passchendael. Peter and Kate were avid correspondents. This letter part of a collection of over ninety surviving letters.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
P.S The reason
I wrote name and
address on back
fearing it may
come back to me
where it is regardless. Katie Caulstown
Dunboyne
Co. Meath
Ireland June 2nd/1916 My Dear Peter I received your ever
welcome letter on Thursday.
I would have sent money that
evening only if teemed rain
for about to his. I enclosed
p.12 for, five whillings within this
letter, hoping your will get it
alright I sent a parcel from
Dublin last Monday with
tobacco and socks and strops
will write a long letter when
I get a permanent address
from you with best luck from
all here. May God bless and
protect you the prayer of
your good fond aunt & sister
